items.q: Why is high IQ a problem for the statistical infrequency definition?
items.correct: It is statistically rare but not abnormal/undesirable.
items.misconception: Because high IQ is actually common.
items.why: Students miss that statistical infrequency lacks a value judgement - it just measures rarity. High IQ is rare but desirable, exposing the definition's limitation.

items.q: Asch's line study primarily demonstrates which type of conformity?
items.correct: Normative social influence (compliance to fit in).
items.misconception: Informational social influence (believing others know better).
items.why: Students assume participants genuinely believed the wrong line was correct. Asch's debriefs showed they knew it was wrong but conformed publicly to avoid rejection - this is normative, not informational.

items.q: What is the capacity of short-term memory according to Miller?
items.why: Students confuse capacity (items) with duration (about 18-30 seconds without rehearsal). Miller's magic number describes how many CHUNKS, not how long.

items.q: Following a direct order from an authority figure is best described as:
items.why: Correct answer: Obedience. “Conformity” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Conforming because you believe others know better than you is:
items.why: Correct answer: ISI. “NSI” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many types of conformity did Kelman propose?
items.why: Exact answer: 3. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
